{
  "decision": "fail",
  "risk": "high",
  "work_stage": "in-service",
  "component_conditions": ["non-compliant", "installed-defective"],
  "percent_complete": 100,

  "confidence": 0.95,

  "order": [],
  "workable": [],
  "findings": [
    "GFCI receptacle wired with line and load terminals reversed, defeating reset and ground-fault protection for downstream loads",
    "GFCI face may show power present but device cannot be properly tested or reset, leaving protected circuits without GFCI coverage",
    "Installation violates manufacturer instructions and NEC 210.8 GFCI protection requirements"
  ],
  "actions": [
    "De-energize circuit at breaker before any rework",
    "Remove GFCI and identify line vs load conductors using tester or meter",
    "Reconnect line conductors to LINE terminals and load conductors to LOAD terminals per manufacturer markings",
    "Test GFCI using built-in TEST button and verify trip and reset function",
    "Verify downstream receptacles on load side are GFCI-protected using a GFCI tester"
  ],
  "rationale": "Line/load reversal on a GFCI receptacle is a critical miswiring defect. When load-side conductors are landed on LINE terminals and line-side conductors on LOAD terminals, the GFCI cannot function as designed — it will not provide ground-fault protection to downstream loads and may not reset or test properly. This leaves the installation non-compliant with NEC 210.8 GFCI protection requirements and creates a shock hazard on what should be protected circuits.",
  "references": ["NEC 210.8", "NEC 110.3(B)"]
}
